  2952→    def exec(
  2953→        self,
  2954→        command: list[str],
  2955→        *,
  2956→        service_context: str | None = None,
  2957→        environment: dict[str, str] | None = None,
  2958→        working_dir: str | pathlib.PurePath | None = None,
  2959→        timeout: float | None = None,
  2960→        user_id: int | None = None,
  2961→        user: str | None = None,
  2962→        group_id: int | None = None,
  2963→        group: str | None = None,
  2964→        stdin: str | bytes | TextIO | BinaryIO | None = None,
  2965→        stdout: TextIO | BinaryIO | None = None,
  2966→        stderr: TextIO | BinaryIO | None = None,
  2967→        encoding: str | None = 'utf-8',
  2968→        combine_stderr: bool = False,
  2969→    ) -> ExecProcess[Any]:
  2970→        r"""Execute the given command on the remote system.
  2971→
  2972→        Two method signatures are shown because this method returns an
  2973→        :class:`ExecProcess` that deals with strings if ``encoding`` is
  2974→        specified (the default ), or one that deals with bytes if ``encoding``
  2975→        is set to ``None``.
  2976→
  2977→        Most of the parameters are explained in the "Parameters" section
  2978→        below, however, input/output handling is a bit more complex. Some
  2979→        examples are shown below::
  2980→
  2981→            # These two lines are related to documentation testing, not exec()
  2982→            >>> import pytest
  2983→            >>> pytest.skip('these examples require a running Pebble')
  2984→
  2985→            # Simple command with no output; just check exit code
  2986→            >>> process = client.exec(['send-emails'])
  2987→            >>> process.wait()
  2988→
  2989→            # Fetch output as string
  2990→            >>> process = client.exec(['python3', '--version'])
  2991→            >>> version, _ = process.wait_output()
  2992→            >>> print(version)
  2993→            Python 3.8.10
  2994→
  2995→            # Fetch both stdout and stderr as strings
  2996→            >>> process = client.exec(['pg_dump', '-s', ...])
  2997→            >>> schema, logs = process.wait_output()
  2998→
  2999→            # Stream input from a string and write output to files

3000→            >>> stdin = 'foo\nbar\n'
  3001→            >>> with open('out.txt', 'w') as out, open('err.txt', 'w') as err:
  3002→            ...     process = client.exec(['awk', '{ print toupper($0) }'],
  3003→            ...                           stdin=stdin, stdout=out, stderr=err)
  3004→            ...     process.wait()
  3005→            >>> open('out.txt').read()
  3006→            'FOO\nBAR\n'
  3007→            >>> open('err.txt').read()
  3008→            ''
  3009→
  3010→            # Real-time streaming using ExecProcess.stdin and ExecProcess.stdout
  3011→            >>> process = client.exec(['cat'])
  3012→            >>> def stdin_thread():
  3013→            ...     for line in ['one\n', '2\n', 'THREE\n']:
  3014→            ...         process.stdin.write(line)
  3015→            ...         process.stdin.flush()
  3016→            ...         time.sleep(1)
  3017→            ...     process.stdin.close()
  3018→            ...
  3019→            >>> threading.Thread(target=stdin_thread).start()
  3020→            >>> for line in process.stdout:
  3021→            ...     print(datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%H:%M:%S'), repr(line))
  3022→            ...
  3023→            16:20:26 'one\n'
  3024→            16:20:27 '2\n'
  3025→            16:20:28 'THREE\n'
  3026→            >>> process.wait()  # will return immediately as stdin was closed
  3027→
  3028→            # Show exception raised for non-zero return code
  3029→            >>> process = client.exec(['ls', 'notexist'])
  3030→            >>> out, err = process.wait_output()
  3031→            Traceback (most recent call last):
  3032→              ...
  3033→            ExecError: "ls" returned exit code 2
  3034→            >>> exc = sys.last_value
  3035→            >>> exc.exit_code
  3036→            2
  3037→            >>> exc.stdout
  3038→            ''
  3039→            >>> exc.stderr
  3040→            "ls: cannot access 'notfound': No such file or directory\n"
  3041→
  3042→        Args:
  3043→            command: Command to execute: the first item is the name (or path)
  3044→                of the executable, the rest of the items are the arguments.
  3045→            service_context: If specified, run the command in the context of
  3046→                this service. Specifically, inherit its environment variables,
  3047→                user/group settings, and working directory. The other exec
  3048→                options will override the service context; ``environment``
  3049→                will be merged on top of the service's.
  3050→            environment: Environment variables to pass to the process.
  3051→            working_dir: Working directory to run the command in. If not set,
  3052→                Pebble uses the target user's $HOME directory (and if the user
  3053→                argument is not set, $HOME of the user Pebble is running as).
  3054→            timeout: Timeout in seconds for the command execution, after which
  3055→                the process will be terminated. If not specified, the
  3056→                execution never times out.
  3057→            user_id: User ID (UID) to run the process as.
  3058→            user: Username to run the process as. User's UID must match
  3059→                user_id if both are specified.
  3060→            group_id: Group ID (GID) to run the process as.
  3061→            group: Group name to run the process as. Group's GID must match
  3062→                group_id if both are specified.
  3063→            stdin: A string or readable file-like object that is sent to the
  3064→                process's standard input. If not set, the caller can write
  3065→                input to :attr:`ExecProcess.stdin` to stream input to the
  3066→                process.
  3067→            stdout: A writable file-like object that the process's standard
  3068→                output is written to. If not set, the caller can use
  3069→                :meth:`ExecProcess.wait_output` to capture output as a string,
  3070→                or read from :meth:`ExecProcess.stdout` to stream output from
  3071→                the process.
  3072→            stderr: A writable file-like object that the process's standard
  3073→                error is written to. If not set, the caller can use
  3074→                :meth:`ExecProcess.wait_output` to capture error output as a
  3075→                string, or read from :meth:`ExecProcess.stderr` to stream
  3076→                error output from the process. Must be None if combine_stderr
  3077→                is ``True``.
  3078→            encoding: If encoding is set (the default is UTF-8), the types
  3079→                read or written to stdin/stdout/stderr are str, and encoding
  3080→                is used to encode them to bytes. If encoding is None, the
  3081→                types read or written are raw bytes.
  3082→            combine_stderr: If true, process's stderr output is combined into
  3083→                its stdout (the stderr argument must be None). If false,
  3084→                separate streams are used for stdout and stderr.
  3085→
  3086→        Returns:
  3087→            A Process object representing the state of the running process.
  3088→            To wait for the command to finish, the caller will typically call
  3089→            :meth:`ExecProcess.wait` if stdout/stderr were provided as
  3090→            arguments to :meth:`exec`, or :meth:`ExecProcess.wait_output` if
  3091→            not.
  3092→
  3093→        Raises:
  3094→            APIError: if an error occurred communicating with pebble, or if the command is not
  3095→                found.
  3096→            ExecError: if the command exits with a non-zero exit code.
  3097→        """
  3098→        with tracer.start_as_current_span('pebble exec') as span:
  3099→            if not isinstance(command, list) or not all(isinstance(s, str) for s in command):
  3100→                raise TypeError(f'command must be a list of str, not {type(command).__name__}')
  3101→            if len(command) < 1:
  3102→                raise ValueError('command must contain at least one item')
  3103→            span.set_attribute('executable', command[0])
  3104→
  3105→            if stdin is not None:
  3106→                if isinstance(stdin, str):
  3107→                    if encoding is None:
  3108→                        raise ValueError('encoding must be set if stdin is str')
  3109→                    stdin = io.BytesIO(stdin.encode(encoding))
  3110→                elif isinstance(stdin, bytes):
  3111→                    if encoding is not None:
  3112→                        raise ValueError('encoding must be None if stdin is bytes')
  3113→                    stdin = io.BytesIO(stdin)
  3114→                elif not hasattr(stdin, 'read'):
  3115→                    raise TypeError('stdin must be str, bytes, or a readable file-like object')
  3116→
  3117→            if combine_stderr and stderr is not None:
  3118→                raise ValueError('stderr must be None if combine_stderr is True')
  3119→
  3120→            body = {
  3121→                'command': command,
  3122→                'service-context': service_context,
  3123→                'environment': environment or {},
  3124→                'working-dir': str(working_dir) if working_dir is not None else None,
  3125→                'timeout': _format_timeout(timeout) if timeout is not None else None,
  3126→                'user-id': user_id,
  3127→                'user': user,
  3128→                'group-id': group_id,
  3129→                'group': group,
  3130→                'split-stderr': not combine_stderr,
  3131→            }
  3132→            resp = self._request('POST', '/v1/exec', body=body)
  3133→            change_id = resp['change']
  3134→            task_id = resp['result']['task-id']
  3135→
  3136→            stderr_ws: _WebSocket | None = None
  3137→            try:
  3138→                control_ws = self._connect_websocket(task_id, 'control')
  3139→                stdio_ws = self._connect_websocket(task_id, 'stdio')
  3140→                if not combine_stderr:
  3141→                    stderr_ws = self._connect_websocket(task_id, 'stderr')
  3142→            except websocket.WebSocketException as e:
  3143→                # Error connecting to websockets, probably due to the exec/change
  3144→                # finishing early with an error. Call wait_change to pick that up.
  3145→                change = self.wait_change(ChangeID(change_id))
  3146→                if change.err:
  3147→                    raise ChangeError(change.err, change) from e
  3148→                raise ConnectionError(f'unexpected error connecting to websockets: {e}') from e
  3149→
  3150→            cancel_stdin: Callable[[], None] | None = None
  3151→            cancel_reader: int | None = None
  3152→            threads: list[threading.Thread] = []
  3153→
  3154→            if stdin is not None:
  3155→                if _has_fileno(stdin):
  3156→                    # Create a pipe so _reader_to_websocket can select() on the
  3157→                    # reader as well as this cancel_reader; when we write anything
  3158→                    # to cancel_writer it'll trigger the select and end the thread.
  3159→                    cancel_reader, cancel_writer = os.pipe()
  3160→
  3161→                    def _cancel_stdin():
  3162→                        os.write(cancel_writer, b'x')  # doesn't matter what we write
  3163→                        os.close(cancel_writer)
  3164→
  3165→                    cancel_stdin = _cancel_stdin
  3166→
  3167→                t = _start_thread(_reader_to_websocket, stdin, stdio_ws, encoding, cancel_reader)
  3168→                threads.append(t)
  3169→                process_stdin = None
  3170→            else:
  3171→                process_stdin = _WebsocketWriter(stdio_ws)
  3172→                if encoding is not None:
  3173→                    process_stdin = io.TextIOWrapper(process_stdin, encoding=encoding, newline='')  # type: ignore
  3174→
  3175→            if stdout is not None:
  3176→                t = _start_thread(_websocket_to_writer, stdio_ws, stdout, encoding)
  3177→                threads.append(t)
  3178→                process_stdout = None
  3179→            else:
  3180→                process_stdout = _WebsocketReader(stdio_ws)
  3181→                if encoding is not None:
  3182→                    process_stdout = io.TextIOWrapper(
  3183→                        process_stdout,  # type: ignore
  3184→                        encoding=encoding,
  3185→                        newline='',
  3186→                    )
  3187→
  3188→            process_stderr = None
  3189→            if not combine_stderr:
  3190→                if stderr is not None:
  3191→                    t = _start_thread(_websocket_to_writer, stderr_ws, stderr, encoding)
  3192→                    threads.append(t)
  3193→                else:
  3194→                    ws = typing.cast('_WebSocket', stderr_ws)
  3195→                    process_stderr = _WebsocketReader(ws)
  3196→                    if encoding is not None:
  3197→                        process_stderr = io.TextIOWrapper(
  3198→                            process_stderr,  # type: ignore
  3199→                            encoding=encoding,
  3200→                            newline='',
  3201→                        )
  3202→
  3203→            process: ExecProcess[Any] = ExecProcess[Any](
  3204→                stdin=process_stdin,  # type: ignore
  3205→                stdout=process_stdout,  # type: ignore
  3206→                stderr=process_stderr,  # type: ignore
  3207→                client=self,
  3208→                timeout=timeout,
  3209→                stdio_ws=stdio_ws,
  3210→                stderr_ws=stderr_ws,
  3211→                control_ws=control_ws,
  3212→                command=command,
  3213→                encoding=encoding,
  3214→                change_id=ChangeID(change_id),
  3215→                cancel_stdin=cancel_stdin,
  3216→                cancel_reader=cancel_reader,
  3217→                threads=threads,
  3218→            )
  3219→            return process
  3220→
  3221→    def _connect_websocket(self, task_id: str, websocket_id: str) -> _WebSocket:
  3222→        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_UNIX, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
  3223→        # Set socket timeout to a short timeout during connection phase, in
  3224→        # case the Pebble side times out (5s), so this side doesn't hang. See:
  3225→        # https://github.com/canonical/operator/issues/1246
  3226→        sock.settimeout(self.timeout)
  3227→        sock.connect(self.socket_path)
  3228→        url = self._websocket_url(task_id, websocket_id)
  3229→        ws: _WebSocket = websocket.WebSocket(skip_utf8_validation=True)  # type: ignore
  3230→        ws.connect(url, socket=sock)
  3231→        # Reset to no timeout so connection can be "long polling" when data is
  3232→        # being received.
  3233→        sock.settimeout(None)
  3234→        return ws
  3235→
  3236→    def _websocket_url(self, task_id: str, websocket_id: str) -> str:
  3237→        base_url = self.base_url.replace('http://', 'ws://')
  3238→        url = f'{base_url}/v1/tasks/{task_id}/websocket/{websocket_id}'
  3239→        return url
  3240→
  3241→    def send_signal(self, sig: int | str, services: Iterable[str]):
  3242→        """Send the given signal to the list of services named.
  3243→
  3244→        Args:
  3245→            sig: Name or number of signal to send, for example ``"SIGHUP"``, ``1``, or
  3246→                ``signal.SIGHUP``.
  3247→            services: Non-empty list of service names to send the signal to.
  3248→
  3249→        Raises:
  3250→            APIError: If any of the services are not in the plan or are not
  3251→                currently running.
  3252→        """
  3253→        with tracer.start_as_current_span('pebble send_signal') as span:
  3254→            if isinstance(services, (str, bytes)) or not hasattr(services, '__iter__'):
  3255→                raise TypeError(
  3256→                    f'services must be of type Iterable[str], not {type(services).__name__}'
  3257→                )
  3258→            services = list(services)
  3259→            for s in services:
  3260→                if not isinstance(s, str):
  3261→                    raise TypeError(f'service names must be str, not {type(s).__name__}')
  3262→
  3263→            if isinstance(sig, int):
  3264→                sig = signal.Signals(sig).name
  3265→
  3266→            body = {
  3267→                'signal': sig,
  3268→                'services': services,
  3269→            }
  3270→            span.set_attributes(body)
  3271→            self._request('POST', '/v1/signals', body=body)
  3272→
  3273→    def get_checks(
  3274→        self, level: CheckLevel | None = None, names: Iterable[str] | None = None
  3275→    ) -> list[CheckInfo]:
  3276→        """Get the check status for the configured checks.
  3277→
  3278→        Args:
  3279→            level: Optional check level to query for (default is to fetch
  3280→                checks with any level).
  3281→            names: Optional list of check names to query for (default is to
  3282→                fetch all checks).
  3283→
  3284→        Returns:
  3285→            List of :class:`CheckInfo` objects.
  3286→        """
  3287→        with tracer.start_as_current_span('pebble get_checks') as span:
  3288→            query: dict[str, Any] = {}
  3289→            if level is not None:
  3290→                query['level'] = level.value
  3291→            if names:
  3292→                query['names'] = list(names)
  3293→            span.set_attributes(query)
  3294→            resp = self._request('GET', '/v1/checks', query)
  3295→            return [CheckInfo.from_dict(info) for info in resp['result']]
  3296→
  3297→    def start_checks(self, checks: Iterable[str]) -> list[str]:

  3300→        .. jujuadded:: 3.6.4

  3311→    def stop_checks(self, checks: Iterable[str]) -> list[str]:

  3314→        .. jujuadded:: 3.6.4

  3325→    def notify(
  3326→        self,
  3327→        type: NoticeType,
  3328→        key: str,
  3329→        *,
  3330→        data: dict[str, str] | None = None,
  3331→        repeat_after: datetime.timedelta | None = None,
  3332→    ) -> str:
  3333→        """Record an occurrence of a notice with the specified options.
  3334→
  3335→        Args:
  3336→            type: Notice type (currently only "custom" notices are supported).
  3337→            key: Notice key; must be in "example.com/path" format.
  3338→            data: Data fields for this notice.
  3339→            repeat_after: Only allow this notice to repeat after this duration
  3340→                has elapsed (the default is to always repeat).
  3341→
  3342→        Returns:
  3343→            The notice's ID.
  3344→        """
  3345→        with tracer.start_as_current_span('pebble notify') as span:
  3346→            span.set_attributes({'type': type.value, 'key': key})
  3347→            body: dict[str, Any] = {
  3348→                'action': 'add',
  3349→                'type': type.value,
  3350→                'key': key,
  3351→            }
  3352→            if data is not None:
  3353→                body['data'] = data
  3354→            if repeat_after is not None:
  3355→                body['repeat-after'] = _format_timeout(repeat_after.total_seconds())
  3356→                span.set_attribute('repeat_after', _format_timeout(repeat_after.total_seconds()))
  3357→            resp = self._request('POST', '/v1/notices', body=body)
  3358→            return resp['result']['id']
  3359→
  3360→    def get_notice(self, id: str) -> Notice:
  3361→        """Get details about a single notice by ID.
  3362→
  3363→        Raises:
  3364→            APIError: if a notice with the given ID is not found (``code`` 404)
  3365→        """
  3366→        with tracer.start_as_current_span('pebble get_notice') as span:
  3367→            span.set_attribute('id', id)
  3368→            resp = self._request('GET', f'/v1/notices/{id}')
  3369→            return Notice.from_dict(resp['result'])

  3371→    def get_notices(
  3372→        self,
  3373→        *,
  3374→        users: NoticesUsers | None = None,
  3375→        user_id: int | None = None,
  3376→        types: Iterable[NoticeType | str] | None = None,
  3377→        keys: Iterable[str] | None = None,
  3378→    ) -> list[Notice]:
  3379→        """Query for notices that match all of the provided filters.
  3380→
  3381→        Pebble returns notices that match all of the filters, for example, if
  3382→        called with ``types=[NoticeType.CUSTOM], keys=["example.com/a"]``,
  3383→        Pebble will only return custom notices that also have key "example.com/a".
  3384→
  3385→        If no filters are specified, return notices viewable by the requesting
  3386→        user (notices whose ``user_id`` matches the requester UID as well as
  3387→        public notices).
  3388→
  3389→        Note that the "after" filter is not yet implemented, as it's not
  3390→        needed right now and it's hard to implement correctly with Python's
  3391→        datetime type, which only has microsecond precision (and Go's Time
  3392→        type has nanosecond precision).
  3393→
  3394→        Args:
  3395→            users: Change which users' notices to return (instead of returning
  3396→                notices for the current user).
  3397→            user_id: Filter for notices for the specified user, including
  3398→                public notices (only works for Pebble admins).
  3399→            types: Filter for notices with any of the specified types.

3400→            keys: Filter for notices with any of the specified keys.
  3401→        """
  3402→        with tracer.start_as_current_span('pebble get_notices') as span:
  3403→            query: dict[str, str | list[str]] = {}
  3404→            if users is not None:
  3405→                query['users'] = users.value
  3406→            if user_id is not None:
  3407→                query['user-id'] = str(user_id)
  3408→            if types is not None:
  3409→                types_value = [(t.value if isinstance(t, NoticeType) else t) for t in types]
  3410→                query['types'] = types_value
  3411→            if keys is not None:
  3412→                keys = list(keys)
  3413→                query['keys'] = keys
  3414→            span.set_attributes(query)
  3415→            resp = self._request('GET', '/v1/notices', query)
  3416→            return [Notice.from_dict(info) for info in resp['result']]

  3418→    def get_identities(self) -> dict[str, Identity]:

  3431→    def replace_identities(self, identities: Mapping[str, IdentityDict | Identity | None]) -> None:

  3450→    def remove_identities(self, identities: Iterable[str]) -> None:

  3464→class _FilesParser:
  3465→    """A limited purpose multi-part parser backed by files for memory efficiency."""
  3466→
  3467→    def __init__(self, boundary: bytes | str):
  3468→        self._response: _FilesResponse | None = None  # externally managed
  3469→        self._part_type: Literal['response', 'files'] | None = None  # externally managed
  3470→        self._headers: email.message.Message | None = None  # externally managed
  3471→        self._files: dict[str, _Tempfile] = {}
  3472→
  3473→        # Prepare the MIME multipart boundary line patterns.
  3474→        if isinstance(boundary, str):
  3475→            boundary = boundary.encode()
  3476→
  3477→        # State vars, as we may enter the feed() function multiple times.
  3478→        self._response_data = bytearray()
  3479→
  3480→        self._max_lookahead = 8 * 1024 * 1024
  3481→
  3482→        self._parser = _MultipartParser(
  3483→            boundary, self._process_header, self._process_body, max_lookahead=self._max_lookahead
  3484→        )
  3485→
  3486→        # RFC 2046 says that the boundary string needs to be preceded by a CRLF.
  3487→        # Unfortunately, the request library's header parsing logic strips off one of
  3488→        # these, so we'll prime the parser buffer with that missing sequence.
  3489→        self._parser.feed(b'\r\n')
  3490→
  3491→    def _process_header(self, data: bytearray):
  3492→        parser = email.parser.BytesFeedParser()
  3493→        parser.feed(data)
  3494→        self._headers = parser.close()
  3495→
  3496→        content_disposition = self._headers.get_content_disposition()
  3497→        if content_disposition != 'form-data':
  3498→            raise ProtocolError(f'unexpected content disposition: {content_disposition!r}')
  3499→
  3500→        name = self._headers.get_param('name', header='content-disposition')
  3501→        if name == 'files':
  3502→            filename = self._headers.get_filename()
  3503→            if filename is None:
  3504→                raise ProtocolError('multipart "files" part missing filename')
  3505→            self._prepare_tempfile(filename)
  3506→        elif name != 'response':
  3507→            raise ProtocolError(f'unexpected name in content-disposition header: {name!r}')
  3508→
  3509→        self._part_type = typing.cast('Literal["response", "files"]', name)
  3510→
  3511→    def _process_body(self, data: bytearray, done: bool = False):
  3512→        if self._part_type == 'response':
  3513→            self._response_data.extend(data)
  3514→            if done:
  3515→                if len(self._response_data) > self._max_lookahead:
  3516→                    raise ProtocolError('response end marker not found')
  3517→                resp = json.loads(self._response_data.decode())
  3518→                self._response = typing.cast('_FilesResponse', resp)
  3519→                self._response_data = bytearray()
  3520→        elif self._part_type == 'files':
  3521→            if done:
  3522→                # This is the final write.
  3523→                outfile = self._get_open_tempfile()
  3524→                outfile.write(data)
  3525→                outfile.close()
  3526→                self._headers = None
  3527→            else:
  3528→                # Not the end of file data yet. Don't open/close file for intermediate writes
  3529→                outfile = self._get_open_tempfile()
  3530→                outfile.write(data)
  3531→
  3532→    def remove_files(self):
  3533→        """Remove all temporary files on disk."""
  3534→        for file in self._files.values():
  3535→            os.unlink(file.name)
  3536→        self._files.clear()
  3537→
  3538→    def feed(self, data: bytes):
  3539→        """Provide more data to the running parser."""
  3540→        self._parser.feed(data)
  3541→
  3542→    def _prepare_tempfile(self, filename: str):
  3543→        tf = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False)  # noqa: SIM115
  3544→        self._files[filename] = tf  # type: ignore # we have a custom protocol for it
  3545→        self.current_filename = filename
  3546→
  3547→    def _get_open_tempfile(self):
  3548→        return self._files[self.current_filename]
  3549→
  3550→    def get_response(self) -> _FilesResponse | None:
  3551→        """Return the deserialized JSON object from the multipart "response" field."""
  3552→        return self._response
  3553→
  3554→    def filenames(self):
  3555→        """Return a list of filenames from the "files" parts of the response."""
  3556→        return list(self._files.keys())
  3557→
  3558→    def get_file(self, path: str | pathlib.PurePath, encoding: str | None) -> _TextOrBinaryIO:
  3559→        """Return an open file object containing the data."""
  3560→        path = str(path)
  3561→        mode = 'r' if encoding else 'rb'
  3562→        # We're using text-based file I/O purely for file encoding purposes, not for
  3563→        # newline normalization.  newline='' serves the line endings as-is.
  3564→        newline = '' if encoding else None
  3565→        file_io = open(  # noqa: SIM115
  3566→            self._files[path].name,
  3567→            mode,
  3568→            encoding=encoding,
  3569→            newline=newline,
  3570→        )
  3571→        # open() returns IO[Any]
  3572→        return typing.cast('_TextOrBinaryIO', file_io)
  3573→
  3574→
  3575→class _MultipartParser:
  3576→    def __init__(
  3577→        self,
  3578→        marker: bytes,
  3579→        handle_header: _HeaderHandler,
  3580→        handle_body: _BodyHandler,
  3581→        max_lookahead: int = 0,
  3582→        max_boundary_length: int = 0,
  3583→    ):
  3584→        r"""Configures a parser for mime multipart messages.
  3585→
  3586→        Args:
  3587→            marker: the multipart boundary marker (i.e. in "\r\n--<marker>--\r\n")
  3588→
  3589→            handle_header(data): called once with the entire contents of a part
  3590→            header as encountered in data fed to the parser
  3591→
  3592→            handle_body(data, done=False): called incrementally as part body
  3593→            data is fed into the parser - its "done" parameter is set to true when
  3594→            the body is complete.
  3595→
  3596→            max_lookahead: maximum amount of bytes to buffer when searching for a complete header.
  3597→
  3598→            max_boundary_length: maximum number of bytes that can make up a part
  3599→            boundary (e.g. \r\n--<marker>--\r\n")
  3600→        """
  3601→        self._marker = marker
  3602→        self._handle_header = handle_header
  3603→        self._handle_body = handle_body
  3604→        self._max_lookahead = max_lookahead
  3605→        self._max_boundary_length = max_boundary_length
  3606→
  3607→        self._buf = bytearray()
  3608→        self._pos = 0  # current position in buf
  3609→        self._done = False  # whether we have found the terminal boundary and are done parsing
  3610→        self._header_terminator = b'\r\n\r\n'
  3611→
  3612→        # RFC 2046 notes optional "linear whitespace" (e.g. [ \t]+) after the boundary pattern
  3613→        # and the optional "--" suffix.  The boundaries strings can be constructed as follows:
  3614→        #
  3615→        #     boundary = \r\n--<marker>[ \t]+\r\n
  3616→        #     terminal_boundary = \r\n--<marker>--[ \t]+\r\n
  3617→        #
  3618→        # 99 is arbitrarily chosen to represent a max number of linear
  3619→        # whitespace characters to help avoid wrongly writing boundary
  3620→        # characters into a (temporary) file.
  3621→        if not max_boundary_length:
  3622→            self._max_boundary_length = len(b'\r\n--' + marker + b'--\r\n') + 99
  3623→
  3624→    def feed(self, data: bytes):
  3625→        """Feeds data incrementally into the parser."""
  3626→        if self._done:
  3627→            return
  3628→        self._buf.extend(data)
  3629→
  3630→        while True:
  3631→            # seek to a boundary if we aren't already on one
  3632→            i, n, self._done = _next_part_boundary(self._buf, self._marker)
  3633→            if i == -1 or self._done:
  3634→                return  # waiting for more data or terminal boundary reached
  3635→
  3636→            if self._pos == 0:
  3637→                # parse the part header
  3638→                if self._max_lookahead and len(self._buf) - self._pos > self._max_lookahead:
  3639→                    raise ProtocolError('header terminator not found')
  3640→                term_index = self._buf.find(self._header_terminator)
  3641→                if term_index == -1:
  3642→                    return  # waiting for more data
  3643→
  3644→                start = i + n
  3645→                # data includes the double CRLF at the end of the header.
  3646→                end = term_index + len(self._header_terminator)
  3647→
  3648→                self._handle_header(self._buf[start:end])
  3649→                self._pos = end
  3650→            else:
  3651→                # parse the part body
  3652→                ii, _, self._done = _next_part_boundary(self._buf, self._marker, start=self._pos)
  3653→                safe_bound = max(0, len(self._buf) - self._max_boundary_length)
  3654→                if ii != -1:
  3655→                    # part body is finished
  3656→                    self._handle_body(self._buf[self._pos : ii], done=True)
  3657→                    self._buf = self._buf[ii:]
  3658→                    self._pos = 0
  3659→                    if self._done:
  3660→                        return  # terminal boundary reached
  3661→                elif safe_bound > self._pos:
  3662→                    # write partial body data
  3663→                    partial_data = self._buf[self._pos : safe_bound]
  3664→                    self._pos = safe_bound
  3665→                    self._handle_body(partial_data)
  3666→                    return  # waiting for more data
  3667→                else:
  3668→                    return  # waiting for more data
  3669→
  3670→
  3671→def _next_part_boundary(buf: bytearray, marker: bytes, start: int = 0) -> tuple[int, int, bool]:
  3672→    """Returns the index of the next boundary marker in buf beginning at start.
  3673→
  3674→    Returns:
  3675→        (index, length, is_terminal) or (-1, -1, False) if no boundary is found.
  3676→    """
  3677→    prefix = b'\r\n--' + marker
  3678→    suffix = b'\r\n'
  3679→    terminal_midfix = b'--'
  3680→
  3681→    i = buf.find(prefix, start)
  3682→    if i == -1:
  3683→        return -1, -1, False
  3684→
  3685→    pos = i + len(prefix)
  3686→    is_terminal = False
  3687→    if buf[pos:].startswith(terminal_midfix):
  3688→        is_terminal = True
  3689→        pos += len(terminal_midfix)
  3690→
  3691→    # Note: RFC 2046 notes optional "linear whitespace" (e.g. [ \t]) after the boundary pattern
  3692→    # and the optional "--" suffix.
  3693→    tail = buf[pos:]
  3694→    for c in tail:
  3695→        if c not in b' \t':
  3696→            break
  3697→        pos += 1
  3698→
  3699→    if buf[pos:].startswith(suffix):
  3700→        pos += len(suffix)
  3701→        return i, pos - i, is_terminal
  3702→    return -1, -1, False
  3703→

   130→def _deepcopy_mutable_fields(obj: object):
   131→    # We don't have a frozendict to freeze any dictionaries, and while we
   132→    # could freeze a list into a tuple, we would break tests that currently
   133→    # assert that they get a list, so we can't do that until 8.0. That means
   134→    # we can't actually freeze the content here, but we can at least
   135→    # disassociate it from the original object.
   136→    for attr, value in obj.__dict__.items():
   137→        if isinstance(value, (dict, list)):
   138→            # We expect that the obj is a frozen dataclass, so have to use
   139→            # object.__setattr__ to bypass the frozen check.
   140→            object.__setattr__(obj, attr, copy.deepcopy(value))
   141→
   142→
   143→# A lot of JujuLogLine objects are created, so we want them to be fast and light.
   144→# Dataclasses define __slots__, so are small, and a namedtuple is actually
   145→# slower to create than a dataclass. A plain dictionary (or TypedDict) would be
   146→# about twice as fast, but less convenient to use.
   147→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
   148→class JujuLogLine:
   149→    """An entry in the Juju debug-log."""
   150→
   151→    level: str
   152→    """The level of the message, for example ``INFO`` or ``ERROR``."""
   153→    message: str
   154→    """The log message."""

   240→def _generate_secret_id():
   241→    # This doesn't account for collisions, but the odds are so low that it
   242→    # should not be possible in any realistic test run.
   243→    secret_id = ''.join(random.choice(string.ascii_lowercase + string.digits) for _ in range(20))  # noqa: S311
   244→    return f'secret:{secret_id}'

   247→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
   248→class Secret:
   249→    """A Juju secret.
   250→
   251→    This class is used for both user and charm secrets.
   252→    """
   253→
   254→    tracked_content: RawSecretRevisionContents
   255→    """The content of the secret that the charm is currently tracking.
   256→
   257→    This is the content the charm will receive with a
   258→    :meth:`ops.Secret.get_content` call."""
   259→
   260→    _: dataclasses.KW_ONLY
   261→
   262→    latest_content: RawSecretRevisionContents | None = None
   263→    """The content of the latest revision of the secret.
   264→
   265→    This is the content the charm will receive with a
   266→    :meth:`ops.Secret.peek_content` call."""
   267→
   268→    id: str = dataclasses.field(default_factory=_generate_secret_id)
   269→    """The Juju ID of the secret.
   270→
   271→    This is automatically assigned and should not usually need to be explicitly set.
   272→    """
   273→
   274→    owner: Literal['unit', 'app', None] = None
   275→    """Indicates if the secret is owned by *this* unit, *this* application, or
   276→    another application/unit.
   277→
   278→    If ``None``, the implication is that read access to the secret has been granted
   279→    to this unit.
   280→    """
   281→
   282→    remote_grants: Mapping[int, set[str]] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
   283→    """Mapping from relation IDs to remote units and applications to which this
   284→    secret has been granted."""
   285→
   286→    label: str | None = None
   287→    """A human-readable label the charm can use to retrieve the secret.
   288→
   289→    If this is set, it implies that the charm has previously set the label.
   290→    """
   291→    description: str | None = None
   292→    """A human-readable description of the secret."""
   293→    expire: datetime.datetime | None = None
   294→    """The time at which the secret will expire."""
   295→    rotate: SecretRotate | None = None
   296→    """The rotation policy for the secret."""
   297→
   298→    # what revision is currently tracked by this charm. Only meaningful if owner=False
   299→    _tracked_revision: int = 1
   300→

300→
   301→    # what revision is the latest for this secret.
   302→    _latest_revision: int = 1
   303→
   304→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   305→        return hash(self.id)
   306→
   307→    def __post_init__(self):
   308→        if self.latest_content is None:
   309→            # bypass frozen dataclass
   310→            object.__setattr__(self, 'latest_content', self.tracked_content)
   311→        _deepcopy_mutable_fields(self)
   312→
   313→    def _set_label(self, label: str):
   314→        # bypass frozen dataclass
   315→        object.__setattr__(self, 'label', label)
   316→
   317→    def _track_latest_revision(self):
   318→        """Set the current revision to the tracked revision."""
   319→        # bypass frozen dataclass
   320→        object.__setattr__(self, '_tracked_revision', self._latest_revision)
   321→        object.__setattr__(self, 'tracked_content', self.latest_content)
   322→
   323→    def _update_metadata(
   324→        self,
   325→        content: RawSecretRevisionContents | None = None,
   326→        label: str | None = None,
   327→        description: str | None = None,
   328→        expire: datetime.datetime | None = None,
   329→        rotate: SecretRotate | None = None,
   330→    ):
   331→        """Update the metadata."""
   332→        # bypass frozen dataclass
   333→        object.__setattr__(self, '_latest_revision', self._latest_revision + 1)
   334→        if content:
   335→            object.__setattr__(self, 'latest_content', content)
   336→        if label:
   337→            object.__setattr__(self, 'label', label)
   338→        if description:
   339→            object.__setattr__(self, 'description', description)
   340→        if expire:
   341→            if isinstance(expire, datetime.timedelta):
   342→                expire = datetime.datetime.now() + expire
   343→            object.__setattr__(self, 'expire', expire)
   344→        if rotate:
   345→            object.__setattr__(self, 'rotate', rotate)
   346→

   407→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
   408→class Network:
   409→    """A Juju network space.
   410→
   411→    Simplifying the Juju "spaces" model, each relation endpoint of the charm is
   412→    associated with a ``Network``, even if charms have not been integrated over
   413→    that endpoint yet.
   414→
   415→    If your charm has a relation ``foo``, then the charm will be able, at
   416→    runtime, to do ``self.model.get_binding("foo").network``. The network you'll
   417→    get by doing so is heavily defaulted and good for most use-cases because the
   418→    charm should typically not be concerned about what IP it gets.
   419→
   420→    If you want to, you can override any of these networks with a custom one by
   421→    passing it to :attr:`State.networks`::
   422→
   423→        state = State(networks={
   424→            Network('foo', [BindAddress([Address('192.0.2.1')])]),
   425→        })
   426→    """
   427→
   428→    binding_name: str
   429→    """The name of the network space."""
   430→    bind_addresses: Sequence[BindAddress] = dataclasses.field(
   431→        default_factory=lambda: [BindAddress([Address('192.0.2.0')])],
   432→    )
   433→    """Addresses that the charm's application should bind to."""
   434→
   435→    _: dataclasses.KW_ONLY
   436→
   437→    ingress_addresses: Sequence[str] = dataclasses.field(
   438→        default_factory=lambda: ['192.0.2.0'],
   439→    )
   440→    """Addresses other applications should use to connect to the unit."""
   441→    egress_subnets: Sequence[str] = dataclasses.field(
   442→        default_factory=lambda: ['192.0.2.0/24'],
   443→    )
   444→    """Subnets that other units will see the charm connecting from."""
   445→
   446→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   447→        return hash(self.binding_name)
   448→
   449→    def __post_init__(self):
   450→        _deepcopy_mutable_fields(self)
   451→
   452→    def _hook_tool_output_fmt(self):
   453→        # dumps itself to dict in the same format the hook command would
   454→        return {
   455→            'bind-addresses': [ba._hook_tool_output_fmt() for ba in self.bind_addresses],
   456→            'egress-subnets': self.egress_subnets,
   457→            'ingress-addresses': self.ingress_addresses,
   458→        }

   464→def _next_relation_id(*, update: bool = True):
   465→    """Get the ID the next relation to be created will get.
   466→
   467→    Pass update=False if you're only inspecting it.
   468→    Pass update=True if you also want to bump it.
   469→    """
   470→    global _next_relation_id_counter
   471→    cur = _next_relation_id_counter
   472→    if update:
   473→        _next_relation_id_counter += 1
   474→    return cur
   475→

   477→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
   478→class RelationBase:
   479→    """Base class for the various types of relation.
   480→
   481→    The only mandatory argument to `Relation` (and other relation types) is
   482→    `endpoint`. The `interface` will be derived from the charm's metadata. When
   483→    fully defaulted, there are no remote units, the remote application is
   484→    called ``remote``, only has a single unit ``remote/0``, and nobody has
   485→    written any data to the databags yet (other than the keys that Juju adds to
   486→    all databags). That is typically the state of a relation when the first unit
   487→    joins it.
   488→    """
   489→
   490→    endpoint: str
   491→    """Relation endpoint name. Must match some endpoint name defined in the metadata."""
   492→
   493→    interface: str | None = None
   494→    """Interface name. Must match the interface name attached to this endpoint in the metadata.
   495→    If left empty, it will be automatically derived from the metadata."""
   496→
   497→    _: dataclasses.KW_ONLY
   498→
   499→    id: int = dataclasses.field(default_factory=_next_relation_id)
   500→    """Juju relation ID. Every new Relation instance gets a unique one,
   501→    if there's trouble, override."""
   502→
   503→    local_app_data: RawDataBagContents = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
   504→    """This application's databag for this relation."""
   505→
   506→    local_unit_data: RawDataBagContents = dataclasses.field(
   507→        default_factory=lambda: _DEFAULT_JUJU_DATABAG.copy(),
   508→    )
   509→    """This unit's databag for this relation."""
   510→
   511→    @property
   512→    def relation_id(self) -> NoReturn:
   513→        """Use `.id` instead of `.relation_id`.
   514→
   515→        :private:
   516→        """
   517→        raise AttributeError('use .id instead of .relation_id')
   518→
   519→    @property
   520→    def _databags(self):
   521→        """Yield all databags in this relation."""
   522→        yield self.local_app_data
   523→        yield self.local_unit_data
   524→
   525→    @property
   526→    def _remote_unit_ids(self) -> tuple[UnitID, ...]:
   527→        """Ids of the units on the other end of this relation."""
   528→        raise NotImplementedError()
   529→
   530→    def _get_databag_for_remote(
   531→        self,
   532→        unit_id: int,
   533→    ) -> RawDataBagContents:
   534→        """Return the databag for some remote unit ID."""
   535→        raise NotImplementedError()
   536→
   537→    def __post_init__(self):
   538→        if type(self) is RelationBase:
   539→            raise RuntimeError(
   540→                'RelationBase cannot be instantiated directly; '
   541→                'please use Relation, PeerRelation, or SubordinateRelation',
   542→            )
   543→
   544→        for databag in self._databags:
   545→            self._validate_databag(databag)
   546→
   547→        _deepcopy_mutable_fields(self)
   548→
   549→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   550→        return hash(self.id)
   551→
   552→    def _validate_databag(self, databag: dict[str, str]):
   553→        if not isinstance(databag, dict):
   554→            raise StateValidationError(
   555→                f'all databags should be dicts, not {type(databag)}',
   556→            )
   557→        for v in databag.values():
   558→            if not isinstance(v, str):
   559→                raise StateValidationError(
   560→                    f'all databags should be Dict[str,str]; found a value of type {type(v)}',
   561→                )
   562→
   563→
   564→_DEFAULT_IP = '192.0.2.0'
   565→_DEFAULT_JUJU_DATABAG = {
   566→    'egress-subnets': _DEFAULT_IP,
   567→    'ingress-address': _DEFAULT_IP,
   568→    'private-address': _DEFAULT_IP,
   569→}

   572→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True, kw_only=True)
   573→class Relation(RelationBase):
   574→    """A relation between the charm and another application."""

   576→    remote_app_name: str = 'remote'
   577→    """The name of the remote application, as in the charm's metadata."""

   579→    # local limit
   580→    limit: int = 1
   581→    """The maximum number of relations on this endpoint."""
   582→
   583→    remote_app_data: RawDataBagContents = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
   584→    """The current content of the application databag."""
   585→    remote_units_data: dict[UnitID, RawDataBagContents] = dataclasses.field(
   586→        default_factory=lambda: {0: _DEFAULT_JUJU_DATABAG.copy()},  # dedup
   587→    )
   588→    """The current content of the databag for each unit in the relation."""
   589→
   590→    remote_model_uuid: str | None = None
   591→    """The remote model's UUID; uses the main model's UUID if not specified."""
   592→
   593→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   594→        return hash(self.id)
   595→
   596→    @property
   597→    def _remote_app_name(self) -> str:
   598→        """Who is on the other end of this relation?"""
   599→        return self.remote_app_name
   600→
   601→    @property
   602→    def _remote_unit_ids(self) -> tuple[UnitID, ...]:
   603→        """Ids of the units on the other end of this relation."""
   604→        return tuple(self.remote_units_data)

   606→    def _get_databag_for_remote(self, unit_id: UnitID) -> RawDataBagContents:
   607→        """Return the databag for some remote unit ID."""
   608→        return self.remote_units_data[unit_id]
   609→
   610→    @property
   611→    def _databags(self):  # type: ignore
   612→        """Yield all databags in this relation."""
   613→        yield self.local_app_data
   614→        yield self.local_unit_data
   615→        yield self.remote_app_data
   616→        yield from self.remote_units_data.values()

   619→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True, kw_only=True)
   620→class SubordinateRelation(RelationBase):
   621→    """A relation to share data between a subordinate and a principal charm."""
   622→
   623→    remote_app_data: RawDataBagContents = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
   624→    """The current content of the remote application databag."""
   625→    remote_unit_data: RawDataBagContents = dataclasses.field(
   626→        default_factory=lambda: _DEFAULT_JUJU_DATABAG.copy(),
   627→    )
   628→    """The current content of the remote unit databag."""
   629→
   630→    remote_app_name: str = 'remote'
   631→    """The name of the remote application that *this unit* is attached to."""
   632→    remote_unit_id: int = 0
   633→    """The ID of the remote unit that *this unit* is attached to."""
   634→
   635→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   636→        return hash(self.id)
   637→
   638→    @property
   639→    def _remote_unit_ids(self) -> tuple[int]:
   640→        """Ids of the units on the other end of this relation."""
   641→        return (self.remote_unit_id,)
   642→
   643→    def _get_databag_for_remote(self, unit_id: int) -> RawDataBagContents:
   644→        """Return the databag for some remote unit ID."""
   645→        if unit_id is not self.remote_unit_id:
   646→            raise ValueError(
   647→                f'invalid unit id ({unit_id}): subordinate relation only has one '
   648→                f'remote and that has id {self.remote_unit_id}',
   649→            )
   650→        return self.remote_unit_data
   651→
   652→    @property
   653→    def _databags(self):
   654→        """Yield all databags in this relation."""
   655→        yield self.local_app_data
   656→        yield self.local_unit_data
   657→        yield self.remote_app_data
   658→        yield self.remote_unit_data
   659→
   660→    @property
   661→    def remote_unit_name(self) -> str:
   662→        """The full name of the remote unit, in the form ``remote/0``."""
   663→        return f'{self.remote_app_name}/{self.remote_unit_id}'

   667→class PeerRelation(RelationBase):

   694→    def _get_databag_for_remote(self, unit_id: UnitID) -> RawDataBagContents:
   695→        """Return the databag for some remote unit ID."""
   696→        return self.peers_data[unit_id]

   750→    The spec will be accessible in the charm via the

   825→def _next_notice_id(*, update: bool = True):
   826→    """Get the ID the next Pebble notice to be created will get.
   827→
   828→    Pass update=False if you're only inspecting it.
   829→    Pass update=True if you also want to bump it.

   904→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True, init=False)
   905→class CheckInfo:
   906→    """A health check for a Pebble workload container."""
   907→
   908→    name: str
   909→    """Name of the check."""
   910→
   911→    level: pebble.CheckLevel | None
   912→    """Level of the check."""
   913→
   914→    startup: pebble.CheckStartup
   915→    """Startup mode of the check."""
   916→
   917→    status: pebble.CheckStatus
   918→    """Status of the check.
   919→
   920→    :attr:`ops.pebble.CheckStatus.UP` means the check is healthy (the number of
   921→    failures is fewer than the threshold), :attr:`ops.pebble.CheckStatus.DOWN`
   922→    means the check is unhealthy (the number of failures has reached the
   923→    threshold), and :attr:`ops.pebble.CheckStatus.INACTIVE` means the check has
   924→    been stopped, so is not currently running.
   925→    """
   926→
   927→    successes: int | None
   928→    """Number of times this check has succeeded.
   929→
   930→    Set this to None to simulate an older version of Pebble which doesn't have
   931→    the ``successes`` field (introduced in Pebble v1.23.0).
   932→    """
   933→
   934→    failures: int
   935→    """Number of failures since the check last succeeded."""
   936→
   937→    threshold: int
   938→    """Failure threshold.
   939→
   940→    This is how many consecutive failures for the check to be considered 'down'.
   941→    """
   942→
   943→    change_id: pebble.ChangeID | None
   944→    """The ID of the Pebble Change associated with this check.
   945→
   946→    Passing ``None`` will automatically assign a new Change ID if the status is
   947→    :attr:`ops.pebble.CheckStatus.UP` or :attr:`ops.pebble.CheckStatus.DOWN`.
   948→    """
   949→
   950→    def __init__(
   951→        self,
   952→        name: str,
   953→        *,
   954→        level: pebble.CheckLevel | str | None = None,
   955→        startup: pebble.CheckStartup = pebble.CheckStartup.ENABLED,
   956→        status: pebble.CheckStatus = pebble.CheckStatus.UP,
   957→        successes: int | None = 0,
   958→        failures: int = 0,
   959→        threshold: int = 3,
   960→        change_id: pebble.ChangeID | None = None,
   961→    ):
   962→        object.__setattr__(self, 'name', name)
   963→        if level is not None:
   964→            level = pebble.CheckLevel(level)
   965→        object.__setattr__(self, 'level', level)
   966→        object.__setattr__(self, 'startup', startup)
   967→        object.__setattr__(self, 'status', status)
   968→        object.__setattr__(self, 'successes', successes)
   969→        object.__setattr__(self, 'failures', failures)
   970→        object.__setattr__(self, 'threshold', threshold)
   971→        if change_id is None:
   972→            if self.status == pebble.CheckStatus.INACTIVE:
   973→                change_id = ''
   974→            else:
   975→                change_id = pebble.ChangeID(_generate_new_change_id())
   976→        object.__setattr__(self, 'change_id', change_id)
   977→
   978→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   979→        return hash(self.name)
   980→
   981→    def _to_ops(self) -> pebble.CheckInfo:
   982→        return pebble.CheckInfo(
   983→            name=self.name,
   984→            level=self.level,
   985→            startup=self.startup,
   986→            status=self.status,
   987→            successes=self.successes,
   988→            failures=self.failures,
   989→            threshold=self.threshold,
   990→            change_id=self.change_id,
   991→        )
   992→

   994→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
   995→class Container:
   996→    """A Kubernetes container where a charm's workload runs."""
   997→
   998→    name: str
   999→    """Name of the container, as found in the charm metadata."""
  1000→
  1001→    _: dataclasses.KW_ONLY
  1002→
  1003→    can_connect: bool = False
  1004→    """When False, all Pebble operations will fail."""
  1005→
  1006→    # This is the base plan. On top of it, one can add layers.
  1007→    # We need to model pebble in this way because it's impossible to retrieve the layers from
  1008→    # pebble or derive them from the resulting plan (which one CAN get from pebble).
  1009→    # So if we are instantiating Container by fetching info from a 'live' charm, the 'layers'
  1010→    # will be unknown. all that we can know is the resulting plan (the 'computed plan').
  1011→    _base_plan: Mapping[str, Any] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
  1012→    # We expect most of the user-facing testing to be covered by this 'layers' attribute,
  1013→    # as it is all that will be known when unit-testing.
  1014→    layers: Mapping[str, pebble.Layer] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
  1015→    """All :class:`ops.pebble.Layer` definitions that have already been added to the container.
  1016→
  1017→    Note that the layers should be added to the dictionary in the order in which they would have
  1018→    been added to Pebble. For layers loaded on Pebble start from the filesystem, this means adding
  1019→    them to the dictionary in alphabetical order by filename. For layers loaded via the Pebble API,
  1020→    this means adding them in the order of the API calls.
  1021→    """
  1022→
  1023→    service_statuses: Mapping[str, pebble.ServiceStatus] = dataclasses.field(
  1024→        default_factory=dict,
  1025→    )
  1026→    """The current status of each Pebble service running in the container."""
  1027→
  1028→    mounts: Mapping[str, Mount] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=dict)
  1029→    """Provides access to the contents of the simulated container filesystem.
  1030→
  1031→    For example, suppose you want to express that your container has:
  1032→
  1033→    * A file ``/home/foo.py``
  1034→    * A directory ``/bin``
  1035→
  1036→    This becomes::
  1037→
  1038→        mounts = {
  1039→            'foo': Mount(location='/home/foo.py', source=pathlib.Path('/path/to/local/foo.py')),
  1040→            'bin': Mount(location='/bin', source=pathlib.Path('/path/to/local/bin')),
  1041→        }
  1042→
  1043→    When your charm runs, the simulated container filesystem will have symlinks to
  1044→    ``/path/to/local/foo.py`` and ``/path/to/local/bin`` at the specified locations.
  1045→
  1046→    If you're testing charm code that uses :meth:`ops.pebble.Client.push` to write files
  1047→    to the container filesystem, make sure to specify source files/directories that can
  1048→    be safely modified.
  1049→    """
  1050→
  1051→    execs: Iterable[Exec] = frozenset()
  1052→    """Simulate executing commands in the container.
  1053→
  1054→    Specify each command the charm might run in the container and an :class:`Exec`
  1055→    containing its return code and any stdout/stderr.
  1056→
  1057→    For example::
  1058→
  1059→        container = Container(
  1060→            name='foo',
  1061→            execs={
  1062→                Exec(['whoami'], return_code=0, stdout='ubuntu'),
  1063→                Exec(
  1064→                    ['dig', '+short', 'canonical.com'],
  1065→                    return_code=0,
  1066→                    stdout='185.125.190.20\\n185.125.190.21',
  1067→                ),
  1068→            }
  1069→        )
  1070→    """
  1071→
  1072→    notices: Sequence[Notice] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)
  1073→    """Any Pebble notices that already exist in the container."""
  1074→
  1075→    check_infos: Iterable[CheckInfo] = frozenset()
  1076→    """All Pebble health checks that have been added to the container."""
  1077→
  1078→    def __hash__(self) -> int:
  1079→        return hash(self.name)
  1080→
  1081→    def __post_init__(self):
  1082→        if not isinstance(self.execs, frozenset):
  1083→            # Allow passing a regular set (or other iterable) of Execs.
  1084→            object.__setattr__(self, 'execs', frozenset(self.execs))
  1085→        _deepcopy_mutable_fields(self)
  1086→
  1087→    def _render_services(self):
  1088→        services: dict[str, pebble.Service] = {}
  1089→        for layer in self.layers.values():
  1090→            for name, service in layer.services.items():
  1091→                if name in services and service.override == 'merge':
  1092→                    services[name]._merge(service)
  1093→                else:
  1094→                    services[name] = service
  1095→        return services
  1096→
  1097→    def _render_checks(self) -> dict[str, pebble.Check]:
  1098→        checks: dict[str, pebble.Check] = {}
  1099→        for layer in self.layers.values():

1100→            for name, check in layer.checks.items():
  1101→                if name in checks and check.override == 'merge':
  1102→                    checks[name]._merge(check)
  1103→                else:
  1104→                    checks[name] = check
  1105→        return checks
  1106→
  1107→    def _render_log_targets(self) -> dict[str, pebble.LogTarget]:
  1108→        log_targets: dict[str, pebble.LogTarget] = {}
  1109→        for layer in self.layers.values():
  1110→            for name, log_target in layer.log_targets.items():
  1111→                if name in log_targets and log_target.override == 'merge':
  1112→                    log_targets[name]._merge(log_target)
  1113→                else:
  1114→                    log_targets[name] = log_target
  1115→        return log_targets
  1116→
  1117→    @property
  1118→    def plan(self) -> pebble.Plan:
  1119→        """The 'computed' Pebble plan.
  1120→
  1121→        This is the base plan plus the layers that have been added on top.
  1122→        You should run your assertions on this plan, not so much on the layers,
  1123→        as those are input data.
  1124→        """
  1125→        # copied over from ops.testing._TestingPebbleClient.get_plan().
  1126→        plan = pebble.Plan(yaml.safe_dump(self._base_plan))
  1127→        services = self._render_services()
  1128→        checks = self._render_checks()
  1129→        log_targets = self._render_log_targets()
  1130→        for name in sorted(services.keys()):
  1131→            plan.services[name] = services[name]
  1132→        for name in sorted(checks.keys()):
  1133→            plan.checks[name] = checks[name]
  1134→        for name in sorted(log_targets.keys()):
  1135→            plan.log_targets[name] = log_targets[name]
  1136→        return plan
  1137→
  1138→    @property
  1139→    def services(self) -> dict[str, pebble.ServiceInfo]:
  1140→        """The Pebble services as rendered in the plan."""
  1141→        services = self._render_services()
  1142→        infos: dict[str, pebble.ServiceInfo] = {}
  1143→        names = sorted(services.keys())
  1144→        for name in names:
  1145→            try:
  1146→                service = services[name]
  1147→            except KeyError:
  1148→                # in pebble, it just returns "nothing matched" if there are 0 matches,
  1149→                # but it ignores services it doesn't recognize
  1150→                continue
  1151→            status = self.service_statuses.get(name, pebble.ServiceStatus.INACTIVE)
  1152→            if service.startup == '':
  1153→                startup = pebble.ServiceStartup.DISABLED
  1154→            else:
  1155→                startup = pebble.ServiceStartup(service.startup)
  1156→            info = pebble.ServiceInfo(
  1157→                name,
  1158→                startup=startup,
  1159→                current=pebble.ServiceStatus(status),
  1160→            )
  1161→            infos[name] = info
  1162→        return infos
  1163→
  1164→    def get_filesystem(self, ctx: Context[Any]) -> pathlib.Path:
  1165→        """Simulated Pebble filesystem in this context.
  1166→
  1167→        Returns:
  1168→            A temporary filesystem containing any files or directories the
  1169→            charm pushed to the container.
  1170→        """
  1171→        return ctx._get_container_root(self.name)
  1172→
  1173→    def get_check_info(self, name: str) -> CheckInfo:
  1174→        """Get the check info for a check by name."""
  1175→        for check_info in self.check_infos:
  1176→            if check_info.name == name:
  1177→                return check_info
  1178→        raise KeyError(f'check-info: {name} not found in the Container')
  1179→
  1180→
  1181→def layer_from_rockcraft(path: pathlib.Path | str) -> pebble.Layer:
  1182→    """Create a layer from a `rockcraft.yaml` file.
  1183→
  1184→    This is a convenience function to create a Pebble layer from a
  1185→    rockcraft.yaml file, that can then be passed to :class:`testing.Container`,
  1186→    rather than duplicating the layer content in the test code. For example::
  1187→
  1188→        container = Container(
  1189→            name='my-container',
  1190→            layers={'rock': layer_from_rockcraft(pathlib.Path('rockcraft.yaml'))},
  1191→        )
  1192→
  1193→    Args:
  1194→        path: Path to the `rockcraft.yaml` file.
  1195→    """
  1196→    if isinstance(path, str):
  1197→        path = pathlib.Path(path)
  1198→    if not path.is_file():
  1199→        raise ValueError(f'rockcraft.yaml file not found at {path}')
  1200→    with path.open('r') as f:
  1201→        rockcraft = yaml.safe_load(f)
  1202→    description = (
  1203→        f'{rockcraft.get("description", "(no description)")} '
  1204→        f'(built from the rockcraft.yaml at {path})'
  1205→    )
  1206→    layer_dict: pebble.LayerDict = {
  1207→        'summary': rockcraft['summary'],
  1208→        'description': description,
  1209→        'services': rockcraft.get('services', {}),
  1210→        'checks': rockcraft.get('checks', {}),
  1211→    }
  1212→    return pebble.Layer(layer_dict)
  1213→

  1225→@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)
  1226→class _EntityStatus:
  1227→    """This class represents StatusBase and should not be interacted with directly."""
  1228→
  1229→    # Why not use StatusBase directly? Because that can't be used with
  1230→    # dataclasses.asdict to then be JSON-serializable.
  1231→
  1232→    name: _RawStatusLiteral
  1233→    message: str = ''
  1234→
  1235→    _entity_statuses: ClassVar[dict[str, type[_EntityStatus]]] = {}
  1236→
  1237→    def __eq__(self, other: Any):
  1238→        if isinstance(other, (StatusBase, _EntityStatus)):
  1239→            return (self.name, self.message) == (other.name, other.message)
  1240→        return super().__eq__(other)
  1241→
  1242→    def __repr__(self):
  1243→        status_type_name = self.name.title() + 'Status'
  1244→        if self.name == 'unknown':
  1245→            return f'{status_type_name}()'
  1246→        return f"{status_type_name}('{self.message}')"
  1247→
  1248→    @classmethod
  1249→    def from_status_name(
  1250→        cls,
  1251→        name: _RawStatusLiteral,
  1252→        message: str = '',
  1253→    ) -> _EntityStatus:
  1254→        """Convert the status name, such as 'active', to the class, such as ActiveStatus."""
  1255→        # Note that this won't work for UnknownStatus.
  1256→        # All subclasses have a default 'name' attribute, but the type checker can't tell that.
  1257→        return cls._entity_statuses[name](message=message)  # type:ignore
  1258→
  1259→    @classmethod
  1260→    def from_ops(cls, obj: StatusBase) -> _EntityStatus:
  1261→        """Convert from the ops.StatusBase object to the matching _EntityStatus object."""
  1262→        return cls.from_status_name(obj.name, obj.message)
  1263→
  1264→    def _to_ops(self) -> StatusBase:
  1265→        """Convert this object to an ops.StatusBase object."""
  1266→        return StatusBase.from_name(self.name, message=self.message)
  1267→

  1479→_next_storage_index_counter = 0  # storage indices start at 0

  1481→
  1482→def _next_storage_index(*, update: bool = True):
  1483→    """Get the index (used to be called ID) the next Storage to be created will get.
  1484→
  1485→    Pass update=False if you're only inspecting it.
  1486→    Pass update=True if you also want to bump it.
  1487→    """
  1488→    global _next_storage_index_counter
  1489→    cur = _next_storage_index_counter
  1490→    if update:
  1491→        _next_storage_index_counter += 1
  1492→    return cur
